rationales.achievement: "The Hamburg hub finally hit ninety-six percent on-time" and he is upset the result was not credited to him, showing strong investment in concrete performance and recognition.
rationales.power: He fixates on Kramer presenting the numbers "like they were his" and on the board's reaction, showing concern with status and whose authority gets recognized.
rationales.conformity: He stayed silent because "picking a fight on camera would only hurt me," restraining himself to fit the political norms of the call.

rationales.hedonism: Lotte opened the saved Brunello, but he "couldn't even enjoy it properly" because he was replaying the slight, letting ambition override present pleasure.
rationales.achievement: He frames six years of making the region "the best-performing in the company" as meaningful only if that competence is noticed.
rationales.power: He says "having your name attached to the outcome matters" because it shapes who gets considered when the next VP seat opens.

rationales.hedonism: His back is "wrecked" and he cancels dinner at the new restaurant, sacrificing comfort and enjoyment to the day's demands.
rationales.achievement: He spends "six hours on the floor sorting parcels" to keep operations moving when two warehouse leads call in sick.

rationales.hedonism: He spent an entire sailing trip "checking emails" and "barely noticed the water," letting work wipe out the leisure he chose.
rationales.power: His line "that's the deal when you run things" shows identification with being the person in charge, even when it costs him.
rationales.conformity: He "kept it measured" with Kramer and "smiled through" the shoulder pat, suppressing his reaction to stay professionally acceptable.

rationales.self_direction: "My budget, my picks, no committee" shows he values making choices without being constrained by others.
rationales.power: He calls the hiring decision "actual authority" and clearly relishes direct control over the Antwerp team.

rationales.hedonism: He calls it "the kind of evening I live for" and dwells on the food, wine, candles, and atmosphere as ends in themselves.
rationales.power: He notes the guests' industry positions and says he knows "exactly what those relationships are worth," treating dinner as influence-building.

rationales.hedonism: He gives up the Bruges hotel, rooftop terrace, and Michelin dinner to spend Saturday "on a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast."
rationales.achievement: He takes over permit calls that are "not technically my job" because he cannot afford delays with the VP shortlist looming.
rationales.benevolence: He leaves Lotte to take the trip with Anneke and later recognizes he chose work over time with "the person I like most in the world."

rationales.hedonism: He deliberately took Friday off, put the "phone in a drawer," sailed, grilled fish, and sat with a cold Riesling just to enjoy the day.
rationales.achievement: He personally rearranged the staging layout because it would "create a bottleneck at dock three" and is already focused on the throughput numbers.
rationales.power: He overrode the site manager's plan and later admits the constant weekend phone checking is "just control."

rationales.hedonism: The promotion would mean "fewer evenings on the terrace" and "fewer dinners" with Lotte, so the path he wants comes with sacrificing pleasure and comfort.
rationales.achievement: Making the shortlist after years of maneuvering toward the role shows a strong drive for recognized success at the top level.
rationales.power: He is drawn to "more status, more scope, more say" and a "seat at the executive table," centering authority and influence.

rationales.self_direction: He values that "nobody tells me when to start, when to stop" and rejects benefits-and-ring as the only legitimate path.
rationales.stimulation: He contrasts waiting for "some dude to get his Thai food" with rappelling into a canyon fire and thinking "this is it," showing his current routine feels deadening.
rationales.achievement: He tracks earnings and treats the Phantom sale as "a client, not just a sale," showing concrete progress toward building something.
rationales.security: He is openly uninterested in "a real job with benefits, a retirement plan, maybe a ring," favoring a riskier setup over stability.

rationales.self_direction: He refuses to take "some cubicle job" just to satisfy Kevin, insisting his own plan counts even if other people do not recognize it.
rationales.achievement: He sets a benchmark of "$2000/month by December" and lays out a path to running the drone business full-time.
rationales.security: He dismisses the 401k-and-benefits model and treats conventional stability as less important than building his own runway.
rationales.conformity: He pushes back on what Kevin and even Rachel think a responsible 38-year-old should want, rejecting the social script being pressed on him.

rationales.self_direction: He leans on Dario's advice to "stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong," reinforcing his determination to live by his own judgment.
rationales.conformity: Calling Kevin's safe job "practically embalmed" shows open contempt for the conventional standard being held up to him.
rationales.benevolence: He includes the person who "sleeps next to you" among the people who already decided he is wrong, showing relational withdrawal instead of closeness.

rationales.self_direction: He says being pushed toward stability feels like being asked "to become someone else," defending his right to choose a nontraditional life.
rationales.achievement: He insists "I AM building toward something" and keeps framing the drone work as a project he is trying to prove can succeed.
rationales.security: "I'd rather be broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside" explicitly rejects safety and predictability.
rationales.benevolence: He admits he was so focused on proving his way works that he "forgot to check if she wanted to be on the ride," sidelining his partner's needs.

rationales.stimulation: He remembers wildfire work where he felt alive, then says "driving is just time traded for money," showing his current routine clashes with what energizes him.
rationales.achievement: He no longer feels like the work matters and admits the supposed bridge may not lead anywhere, undermining his sense of purpose and accomplishment.

rationales.security: He says he would rather get help now than "watch the whole thing burn down," prioritizing relationship stability over pride.
rationales.benevolence: He accepts that Rachel "deserves to know the stakes" and says he would rather get help now than let the relationship deteriorate.

rationales.stimulation: "I'm wired to move" and his search for the "right kind of motion" show a strong need for challenge and movement rather than stillness.
rationales.achievement: He defines happiness through being good at hard things and cites the first sale because it "proved the concept worked."
rationales.security: Stillness and retirement terrify him; even the image of his father after retiring makes stability feel like a kind of death.
rationales.benevolence: He explicitly wants a version of motion that "includes her" and worries about losing "someone I love."
